macro_segments.title: The Scope of Bal Yera'eh Bal Yimatze and the Case of Non-Jewish Deposits

micro_segments.title: Extension of the prohibition to chametz outside the house
The apparent contradiction: hiding chametz and accepting deposits from non-Jews
Resolution through the principle of responsibility: davar hagorem lemamon
The case of the soldiers and Rava's ruling to Mechuza residents
Debate on whether davar hagorem lemamon is itself a halachic principle or requires a Biblical source
The case where chametz will be returned: why lo yimatze still applies

macro_segments.title: Ownership of a Non-Jew in Animals Designated for Gifts and Taxes

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the question of partial non-Jewish ownership in bechor
The case of arnona (tax): can you pay it off in money?
Rava's position: exemption from bechor despite tax lien
The distinction between behemah and isah: publicity and marit ayin
Resolution: ayin lei kala (behemah is publicized) vs. leit lei kala (bread is not)

macro_segments.title: The Non-Jew in the Jewish House: Deposits, Designated Space, and Ownership

micro_segments.title: A non-Jew enters with chametz: no obligation to remove if not accepted as deposit
If designated a space: Rashi's view that no responsibility is accepted
Tosafot's alternative: responsibility accepted but space designated anyway
The question of lo yimatze and the distinction between first and second cases
Rav Ashi's interpretation: lo yimatze applies to the second case (designated space)
The question of sekhirus kanya: does designating space amount to rental?
The concern about housing non-Jews as dwelling: idolatry implications
Resolution: the Torah's language lo yimatze excludes this case from the rental prohibition
The principle of matsui beyadecha: readily accessible versus designated space

macro_segments.title: Practical Cases on Pesach: Covering Chametz on Yom Tov and Identifying Non-Jewish Chametz

micro_segments.title: If you find chametz on Pesach (yom tov), it is muktzeh and must be covered
Exception: if you declare it hekdesh, no need to cover (it has inherent sanctity)
Non-Jewish chametz requires physical separation (mechitzah) for recognition (heker)

macro_segments.title: The Timing of Bi'ur Chametz: Thirty Days and the Principle of Intent to Return

micro_segments.title: If leaving 30+ days before Pesach, no obligation to burn if not returning
Abaye's position: the question is intent to return, not time elapsed
Rava's restatement: if you intend to return, must burn even from Rosh Hashanah
The case of making your house a storehouse: same principle applies
Rashi's explanation: once Pesach arrives, chametz is issur hana'ah and leaves your possession

macro_segments.title: The Obligation to Perform Bitul Even After Bedika: The Problem of the Gluska Yafa

micro_segments.title: The problem: on Pesach itself, chametz is issur hana'ah and out of one's possession, yet one remains liable

macro_segments.title: The Timing of Bi'ur Chametz: Thirty Days and the Principle of Intent to Return (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's position: lav hanitak la'aseh—non-performance of tashbitu does not violate the prohibition until Pesach ends

macro_segments.title: The Origin of the Thirty-Day Period and Hermeneutical Principles

micro_segments.title: The thirty-day period derives from the period between Pesach Rishon and Pesach Sheini
The assumption that within thirty days of Pesach, one is already involved in Pesach matters
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel's view: two weeks suffice based on Rosh Chodesh
The broader principle: ein mukdam u'meuchar ba'Torah—Torah's chronological order
Rav Papa's limitation: chronological order applies only within a single topic (chad inyana)
The hermeneutical consequences: how klal u'prat and ribuy u'mi'ut depend on chronological ordering

macro_segments.title: The Obligation to Perform Bitul Even After Bedika: The Problem of the Gluska Yafa (continued)

micro_segments.title: One who searches must still perform bitul, even though crumbs are insignificant
The concern: finding a nice roll (gluska yafa) and becoming negligent
Rav Elazar: two things not in one's reshut yet treated as if they are—a pit in public domain and chametz after six hours

macro_segments.title: The Mechanics of Bitul Chametz: Hefker versus Mental Negation

micro_segments.title: Rashi's view: bitul is stated as ke'afra d'ara (like dust), not hefker
Tosafot's view: bitul operates as hefker, making the chametz ownerless
Ran's objection: hefker is ineffective because no one else can legally take it on Pesach
Ran's alternative: bitul prevents the chametz from returning to your possession, treating it as if destroyed mentally
The practical formulation: both yibatil and lihyot hefker ke'afra d'ara to cover all positions

topical_tags.term: Ke'afra d'ara (like dust of the earth)
